# Transcrição: 03-What is a Story.

- **Fonte:** 03-What is a Story.
- **Duração:** 17:48
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, it's time to learn how to tell a story. The first thing we have to understand is what is a story, because honestly, most people don't really understand what a story is and what a story isn't. Many people are under the impression that a story is a simple accounting of your life. Something happened to me over a certain period of time, and now I'm going to tell you that thing. That is not a story. That is often a painful process for your listener. In the worst case scenario, it's a vacation story. Your friend goes off on vacation and then returns and you say, how was your vacation? And they begin saying, well, we took off on Sunday night from New York and we flew out to Bermuda. And then when we landed and then you realize, oh my gosh, they're going to give me a play by play of the entire vacation. Thinking they're telling you a story when really what they're doing is reliving their vacation at your expense. These are not stories. The stuff that happens to you in your life, even the amazing, unbelievable, ridiculous things, they don't amount to stories. A story is about one thing, change. Change over time. It comes in two different forms. It's either transformation, meaning I used to be one kind of a person and then some stuff happened. That's the story. And now I'm a different kind of person or more common realization. I used to think one thing and then some stuff happened, the story. And now I think a new thing. That's all a story really is. In order for a story to touch the heart and mind of another person, it can't just be a series of events that took place over time. It has to be I have fundamentally changed as a human being in an enormous or a tiny way. As long as there is change over time, you have yourself a story. In the spoon of power, I once was a person who thought a spoon was just a spoon and then some stuff happened and I became a person who understood that my spoon of power was the most valuable teaching tool I've ever had. In my bad blood draw story, I once was a person who thought blood draws would always be terrifying experiences for me. And then some stuff happened and I discovered that maybe they don't have to be as bad as I thought. That is change over time and that is how all stories work. Whether that story is a story that you're telling or it's a story that you're reading about or seeing in the movies. I believe that when we tell stories, what we're really trying to do is to create a movie in the mind of our audience. We're actually trying to get our audience to forget who they are and where they are and activate that mind's eye, that third eye inside them so that they can see the movie that we're projecting to them.

## [03:03 - 06:07]

And I really believe that happens. I believe it happens in the movie theater. It happens in the book. Maybe it even happened when you were listening to the spoon of power. Maybe for a moment, you disappeared completely. Your existence disappeared completely and you were in a classroom watching a little boy stand on a chair and put a spoon around his teacher's neck. In the best versions of storytelling, I believe that happens. In a theater, I think it happens quite often. If you've ever seen the movie Titanic, for example, it's my favorite example. Titanic is a movie about an actual event that took place and it's populated by real people, the captain of the ship, the designer of the ship, all of those wealthy people who were aboard. They were all actual historical people who were aboard that ship and many, many, many of them died in the ocean. And yet Titanic is a story about two people, Jack and Rose, Leonardo DiCaprio and Kate Winslet. Those two people did not actually exist in real life and we know that when we're watching the movie. We know that that's Leonardo DiCaprio. He was in What's Eating Gilbert Grape like six months ago and he's going to be in Revolutionary Road six months from now and that's Kate Winslet. I've never seen her before but she's surely going to go on to have a great career. We know that those two actors were not aboard the Titanic. We know they're not cold. We know they're fine and we know they're millions of dollars richer because of the success of this film and yet we cry when they die. We don't cry when the band plays on and sinks into the water, even though that band really did play on and sink into the water. We don't die when the ship's captain goes down with the boat, even though the ship's captain really went down with the boat. We cry when the fictional characters die. Why? Because we forget who we are and we forget where we are. That is the magic of story. When we tell stories, we're putting movies into the minds of our audience. We're trying to get them to see what we want them to see and so that's why I think the studying of movies is essential to telling the stories that we want to tell. Think about Titanic for example, right? That's a story actually about Rose. Rose is the person in that movie that changes over time. Jack is a self-actualized character at the beginning of the movie. He doesn't have to change in any way. He's perfectly happy with who he is, which is why he has to die by the way. Characters that begin movies, self-actualized, often die by the end of the movie because that's the only place they have to go. But Rose has changed to make. Rose is trapped in this world of wealth and structure and limitation. She's being told who to marry. She's being told what to do. By the end of the movie, she's let go of all of her wealth. At a moment when she could re-engage with society, she instead

## [06:07 - 09:08]

decides to hide and go live her own life. That movie is not about Jack and Rose, it's about Rose. She is the person that undergoes change over time and that's how all great movies and almost all movies work. Let's think about a movie like the very first Star Wars movie. Two protagonists in that movie, Han Solo and Luke Skywalker. Luke Skywalker is a boy on a desert planet believing that someday he's going to fly a spaceship into space and defeat the Empire. And then he meets Obi-Wan Kenobi who teaches him about the force. Luke is a person who doesn't have religion at the beginning of the movie. He believes in spaceships and blasters and technology. He's introduced to religion by Obi-Wan Kenobi and at the end of the movie, at the moment when he's going to defeat the Empire with technology, he turns off his targeting computer and instead uses the force. Star Wars is a story about a boy who doesn't have religion and then finds religion and uses that religion to defeat evil. In that same movie Han Solo at the beginning of the movie is a scoundrel. He's a smuggler. He's a guy who's taking Luke and Obi-Wan to Alderaan for money only. That is his sole purpose in life. He is a person who is self-centered and selfish. At the end of the movie when he has a chance to leave to go pay his debts to get out of the trouble he's in, instead at the last minute he reappears. He blasts Darth Vader out of the way so that Luke can defeat the Empire. Han Solo goes from selfish to selfless. Both of those characters undergo significant change over time and that's what the movie is about. We think it's about stormtroopers and Darth Vader and spaceships and science fiction but it's not. It's about religion and it's about selflessness. All movies are like this. Think about the first Indiana Jones movie, Indiana Jones and the Raiders of the Lost Ark. Beginning of that movie Indiana Jones played by Harrison Ford, he's in a classroom. He's teaching some students when the government comes into his classroom and says the Nazis are looking for the Ark of the Covenant. What does that mean? Indiana Jones explains it's believed that an army that carries the Ark of the Covenant at the front of the army can never be defeated. He says it's mumbo jumbo, it's hocus pocus, it's ancient nonsense. He doesn't believe in the religion behind the Ark of the Covenant but he agrees to search for it because he's an archaeologist, he's a scientist, he wants to find this thing. By the end of the movie Indiana Jones is tied to a post with the woman he loves, Marion and the Nazis are about to open the Ark of the Covenant. He's about to see the thing he's been waiting all of his life to see and in that moment he says close your eyes, Marion

## [09:09 - 12:12]

and he closes his own eyes. Why? Because suddenly Indiana Jones believes in religion. He believes in the power of the Ark of the Covenant. He understands that if his eyes stay open he will die, that God will punish him for keeping his eyes open. Indiana Jones, Raiders of the Lost Ark, it's not about snakes and Nazis and whips and deserts, it's a story about religion. It's a man who doesn't have religion and then finds religion and that's why when you leave that movie you feel good about it. You don't know on sort of a surface level that Luke Skywalker found religion and Han Solo found selflessness and that Indiana Jones found religion. You don't know that on a conscious level but somewhere deep down you know something really important happened. You understand that change has taken place over time, that I've watched a human being grow in a significant way and that's why we leave those movies feeling so good. My favorite example is Jurassic Park, the very first Jurassic Park. You think of that as the movie about dinosaurs but it's not. It's actually a movie about a man who can't be with the woman he loves because they can't agree on whether they're going to have children. I know that sounds crazy but follow me. Beginning of the movie there's two archaeologists, Ellie Sadler and Dr. Grant and the two of them are at a dig site and they're sort of looking at a dinosaur. They're looking at a velociraptor skeleton and a little boy comes over. There should not be a little boy at a dig site like this but whatever, he's needed there for the story and he looks at the dinosaur fossil and he says, looks like a giant chicken to me and Grant takes this fossilized claw from his pocket, a velociraptor claw, one of his most prized possessions and he says, giant chicken. He says, that thing would rip your belly open and eat you before you were even dead. Not the kind of thing you want to say to a 10 year old boy but then they walk away and as they're walking away, Ellie says to Grant, why did you do that? And he says, that's what you want. You want one of those, one of those small creatures and she says, well I don't want that boy but yes, I'd like to have one of those kids someday and that's what the movie's about. The movie is about the fact that the two of these people are together but not entirely together. She wants to have kids and he doesn't. What happens over the course of the movie? Should we be surprised that the one person who ends up in Jurassic Park taking care of two children is the guy who doesn't want to have kids? Dr. Grant ends up in the jungle with these two kids desperately trying to keep them alive and while he does, he gets closer and closer to them. The boy, his name is Tim, is just like Dr. Grant. Cracks jokes all the time, daring,

## [12:12 - 15:18]

curious and the girl, Lexi, she's got this like old soul. She understands technology in ways that a kid should not really understand technology but she's a sweet girl and throughout the movie, you can watch them not only become emotionally closer but even physically closer. The end of that movie actually happens in a tree. Grant and the two kids have climbed a tree. They've just fed some vegetable eating dinosaurs and it's nighttime so he pulls them in like a father would hold two of his own children and Tim cracks a joke, the best joke of the movie and Grant laughs his loudest laugh and then Tim falls asleep in his arms and then Lexi says, what if the mean dinosaurs come, the meat eating dinosaurs come back and Grant says, I'll stay up all night for you and she says, all night and he says, don't worry, all night and then he pulls her in as close as you can hold a kid, two children in his arms falling asleep like he's a father and as he does so, that claw, that fossilized claw that he threatened the boy with at the beginning of the movie, it falls out of his pocket and lands on the ground. Quite literally, he has released the thing that once had value to him because he now is holding the things that have the most value to him. It is a movie about a man who has to learn to love children so he can love the woman he wants to be with. That's what Jurassic Park is about. Now that's not the actual end of the movie because they're still trapped on an island, a couple more people have to die, a helicopter has to arrive, but that is the real end of the movie, which is why when you leave that movie, you think that was a good movie. It's not because the special effects were extraordinary. It's not because it's a movie about dinosaurs because many, many movies have been made about dinosaurs. It's because Spielberg understands something. He understands that you'll go to the movie to see the dinosaurs. You'll buy your popcorn and your soda thinking, I'm going to watch an exciting movie about dinosaurs, but Spielberg also understands it can't be about the dinosaurs. There has to be something real. There has to be change taking place over time. We need to watch a human being grow in an enormous or a tiny way. We have to see change over time to touch the hearts and the minds of people. That is what a story is. A story is not about the crazy thing that happened last night. It's not about the weird thing that happened when you were 17. This is good for you because what it tells you is that stories don't have to be about insanity. Stories don't have to be about going through windshields of cars or ending up homeless. They don't have to be about the time you were on trial for the crime you didn't commit. All you have to do is change in some way. You have to see

## [15:18 - 17:48]

the world in a new way, see yourself in a new way, see your company in a new way. Think about your mother differently. Think about politics differently. You have to just change as a person. Wow, I used to be kind of lazy and now I'm not quite so lazy. That's a story. The good news is you don't need dinosaurs, you don't need a sinking ship, you don't need snakes and Nazis, and you don't need Darth Vader. Those are the kinds of stories that bring people in so that they eat popcorn and drink soda and feel good about what's happening on the screen. But the stories that we tell for ourselves, for our companies, for our businesses, they don't need to be enormous. They just need to be stories. They have to be about change over time. That's what we're looking for. Once you understand what a story is, you can begin telling highly effective stories. One more thing. Start paying attention to the stories you're hearing in your life. Start paying attention to the movies that you watch. A couple years ago, my children and I sat down and we watched a movie called Bolt. It's a Pixar movie. I had never seen it before, never heard of it before. We sat down to watch it. About 30 seconds into that movie, I paused and I turned to my daughter and son and said, this is a movie about a dog who needs to find a home and needs to find a friend as well. And I think the friend will be a cat. And at the end, the dog and the cat will have a new home and they will be together. And then the movie continued and that's exactly what it was. At the end of the movie, my daughter turned to me and she said, how did you know that? And I said, it was the opening shot. I only needed one second to know. The opening shot of that movie, it's a dog in the window of a pet shop. No other animals. That dog is all alone watching people walk by looking sad. And so I said to myself, this is a dog who needs a home. This is a dog who doesn't have any friends. Therefore, that must be what's going to happen at the end of the story. And it was true. I understand what stories are. I understand how they're built. I understand that stories are changed over time. Regardless of the story, they are about change over time. That's what you're looking for. Now that you understand what a story is, you'll already be a better storyteller. Now let's start putting stories together.
